metadata.summary: Deterministic process for building a schedule from WBS: define activities, sequence with dependencies, PERT estimation, CPM critical path, buffer placement.

content.title: Schedule Development
content.summary: A deterministic process for building a project schedule from WBS work packages: define activities, sequence with dependency types (FS/FF/SS/SF), estimate durations using three-point PERT, run forward and backward pass to compute Early Start/Early Finish/Late Start/Late Finish per activity, identify the critical path (zero float), and place buffers at merge points and project end using Critical Chain methods rather than per-task padding.

why.p: Without a critical path, delays cascade invisibly. Near-critical paths (float less than 2 days) become critical after one slip and are invisible without slack analysis. Per-task padding hides slack and creates Student Syndrome and Parkinson's Law effects. PERT three-point estimation forces pessimistic cases into the conversation before they happen. The schedule is the primary coordination artefact for multi-resource projects.

LLMs default to wildly optimistic durations because training data emphasises happy paths; pessimistic values must be hand-injected. PERT mean assumes a beta distribution but software task duration is heavy-tailed lognormal — P50 schedule will slip approximately 70% of the time. Critical path "longest chain" is brittle: a near-critical path 1 day shorter becomes critical after one slip and is invisible without slack analysis. Resource leveling is NOT part of CPM — same person on parallel critical-path tasks blows the schedule silently. Multi-project portfolios with shared resources need RCPSP, not CPM; agents almost never solve this correctly. Buffer placement (Critical Chain / Goldratt) gives better statistical protection than padding each task; most agent outputs pad tasks instead, hiding slack.

list.item: Building the initial schedule from a WBS at kickoff (activity definition through CPM baseline)
Re-baselining after a change-control event (scope add, vendor slip, milestone shift)
Producing PERT three-point estimates for stakeholder communications
Solopreneur weekly capacity planning across multiple parallel projects
Pure agile teams with fixed-cadence sprints — velocity and roadmap replace CPM; critical path is overhead
Highly creative / R&D work where activity duration is unknowable — use rolling-wave or Kanban instead
Tasks under 2 weeks with a single owner — a checklist suffices
Fixed-deadline projects where you back-plan from end date — use reverse-pass / time-boxed scope planning

section.name: step-1-define-activities
section.p: Convert each WBS work package into 3-7 specific activities with a clear deliverable. Fewer activities hide risk; more create false-precision overhead. Name activities as verb+noun ("Design auth flow", not "Auth").

section.name: step-2-sequence-activities
section.p: Assign predecessor relationships with dependency types:

list.item: FS (Finish-to-Start) — predecessor must finish before successor starts; default type, use unless explicitly justified otherwise
FF (Finish-to-Finish) — both activities finish together (documentation with development)
SS (Start-to-Start) — both start together (testing starts when development starts)
SF (Start-to-Finish) — rare; use only when required and document why

section.name: step-3-pert-three-point-estimation
section.p: For each activity collect Optimistic (O), Most Likely (M), and Pessimistic (P) durations. Compute expected duration and standard deviation:

list.item: Expected duration = (O + 4M + P) / 6
Standard deviation = (P - O) / 6
Example: O=3d, M=5d, P=10d then Expected = (3 + 20 + 10)/6 = 5.5d

section.name: step-4-forward-and-backward-pass
section.p: Forward pass: traverse activities in topological order, propagating Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF). ES of first activity = 0; ES of successor = max(EF of all predecessors + lag). EF = ES + duration.

Backward pass: traverse in reverse topological order. LF of last activity = EF of last activity. LF of predecessor = min(LS of all successors - lag). LS = LF - duration. Total float = LF - EF = LS - ES. Critical path: all activities with float = 0.

section.name: step-5-buffers
section.p: Critical Chain buffering: place one project buffer at the project end (typically 50% of critical path standard deviation sum). Place feeding buffers where non-critical paths join the critical path. Do not pad individual activities — hidden per-task slack always gets consumed (Parkinson's Law).

rule.statement: Use FS dependencies by default; use SS/FF/SF only when truly required and document the justification in the activity list.
rule.rationale: Most "we can parallelize" claims fail at integration; FS is conservative and explicit about ordering.

rule.statement: Track near-critical paths (float less than 2 days) explicitly alongside the critical path; report them at every status review.
rule.rationale: A near-critical path one day shorter than the critical path becomes critical after one slip and is invisible without slack analysis.

rule.statement: Lock the schedule baseline after kickoff; re-baseline only on approved change requests, never on standup updates.
rule.rationale: Constant re-baselining hides slip from stakeholders; tracking variance against the original baseline reveals true schedule health.

rule.statement: Effort hours are not duration days; model resource availability explicitly (a 40-hour task for a 50%-available resource takes 10 days, not 5).
rule.rationale: Effort-vs-duration confusion is the most common single source of schedule slip in agent-generated schedules.

rule.statement: Reject any schedule where activities have no explicit predecessors defined in the source data; agents must not infer dependencies from prose.
rule.rationale: Hallucinated dependencies produce incorrect critical paths and conceal missing information that the PM must resolve.

rule.statement: Use business-calendar libraries for all date arithmetic; never compute working days with raw date math.
rule.rationale: Raw date math silently skips weekends, public holidays, and half-days, producing schedule dates that do not match calendar reality.

antipattern.description: Padding individual activity durations instead of using project-end and feeding buffers.
antipattern.reason: Per-task padding hides slack, triggers Parkinson's Law (work expands to fill allotted time), and makes the critical path invisible.

antipattern.description: Reporting only "the critical path" as a single chain when multiple paths tie at the same finish.
antipattern.reason: Multi-path criticality means all paths with float less than or equal to 0 must be managed; reporting only one gives false security.

antipattern.description: LLM produces wildly optimistic durations because training data emphasises happy paths.
antipattern.reason: Force three-point estimation with explicit pessimistic values from the project team; reject point estimates for uncertain activities.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: The agent is a deterministic schedule compiler. Feed it WBS work packages with effort + role + dependency edges; it emits an activity-on-node graph, runs forward + backward pass for ES/EF/LS/LF, computes total/free float, identifies critical path, and outputs Gantt-renderable JSON. A second agent runs Monte Carlo over 3-point durations to produce P50/P80/P95 finish dates. Humans approve buffer sizing and resource constraints before the schedule becomes the baseline.

Recommended subagents: schedule-builder (ingests wbs.yaml + dependencies.yaml, outputs CPM JSON with float per activity), monte-carlo-scheduler (runs N=10000 simulations over PERT triples, outputs date distribution + sensitivity), resource-leveler (checks no resource is double-booked; flags conflicts with proposed re-sequencing), faion-sdd-executor (once the schedule baseline is approved, drives task-by-task execution under quality gates).

section.name: prompt-pattern
section.p: Inputs: wbs.yaml with [{id, name, effort_hours, role, predecessors:[{id, type:FS|FF|SS|SF, lag_days}]}], calendar.yaml with working days/holidays per resource, start_date YYYY-MM-DD.

Output JSON: { "activities": [{id, ES, EF, LS, LF, total_float, free_float, critical:bool}], "critical_path": [id...], "project_finish_p50": "...", "p80": "...", "p95": "...", "near_critical_paths": [[id...]], "resource_conflicts": [...] }.
Rules: No invented durations; if missing, return error not guess. Lag in business days, never calendar days unless calendar.yaml says otherwise. Reject WBS containing cycles; report the cycle.

list.item: Use Critical Chain buffering (one project buffer at end, feeding buffers at merge points) — not per-task padding. Hidden slack always gets used (Parkinson's Law).
Track schedule performance index (SPI from EVM) weekly, not just milestone hits — reveals slip earlier.
Lock the baseline once; re-baselining hides slip from stakeholders. Track variance against the original.
Keep the WBS-to-activity ratio at approximately 3-7 activities per work package; finer creates a fake-precision schedule, coarser hides risk.
Identify near-critical paths (float less than 2 days) explicitly; one slip promotes them. Without this you only see issues post-mortem.
Use FS dependencies by default; SS/FF/SF only when truly required and document why. Most "we can parallelize" claims fail integration.
Effort vs duration confusion: 40 effort-hours is NOT 5 working days for a 50%-loaded resource (it's 10). Always model availability.
LLMs hallucinate dependencies they cannot verify; require explicit predecessors: in input, never let agent infer them from prose.
"How long does X take?" prompts return median industry numbers; force a 3-point with sources or refuse.
Calendar arithmetic is a frequent agent bug (skipping weekends, public holidays, half-days). Use business_calendar libraries, never raw date math.
Recursive/automatic re-scheduling on every standup creates instability — humans need a stable plan to commit against. Re-baseline only on change-control events.
Critical path agents often miss multi-path criticality (two paths tie at the same finish); print all paths whose float less than or equal to 0, not "the" path.
Resource calendars in different timezones are a silent slip generator; always assert tz on every date.

template.purpose: Activity table with ID, duration, dependencies, and resource columns

script.purpose: CPM forward/backward pass using networkx; validates DAG, computes ES/EF/LS/LF and float per activity
